_pass: Pass 1 + worked examples v2 + LA trim
title: What statistics is, really
deck: Two kinds of statistics. Descriptive describes the data we have; inferential makes claims about a population from a sample. Naming the difference is the move that makes the rest of the chapter make sense.
objectives: Differentiate between descriptive and inferential statistics, and identify which kind of claim a given statement is making.

content.text: A poll headline scrolls past during the evening news: “45% of Americans favor the new bill, ±3%.” Two sentences worth of math are hiding inside that single line. The 45% is what the pollsters actually measured in a specific group of people they called; the ±3% is what they claim the percentage would be if they had called every adult in the country. The two numbers answer two different questions, and both questions are settled by what we call “statistics.”

This chapter develops the math behind both kinds of statements. The descriptive side is settled in Sections 5.2 and 5.3, where we compute the center and the spread of a data set we already have in hand. The inferential side is settled in Sections 5.5 and 5.6, where the normal distribution and its Empirical Rule make it possible to project from a sample to a population. Section 5.4 sits in between, showing how to read the standard data displays that summarize either kind of statistical claim visually.

Statistics begins with a data set: a list of numerical measurements collected by an experiment, a survey, or an observational study. Once a data set is in hand, two very different kinds of statistical claims can be made about it. A descriptive claim summarizes the data set itself: the average weight of these ten patients, the median income of those eighty households, the most common score on this twenty-five-student exam. Descriptive claims are exact for the data they describe, with no margin of error, because they are about a known group whose members can be counted one by one.

An inferential claim, by contrast, uses the data set as a stand-in — a sample — for a much larger group that was not measured directly. The pollster who calls 1,000 voters and reports a 45% approval rate is not really making a claim about those 1,000 individuals; their interest is in projecting that finding onto the roughly 150 million Americans who will eventually vote. The leap from the sample to the population is the inferential move, and it always carries a margin of error, because the sample may not perfectly mirror the larger group.

content.caption: Figure 5.1. The two halves of statistics. The descriptive side reports an exact summary of the data actually collected; the inferential side stretches that summary into a claim about a larger, unmeasured population, with a margin of error attached.

content.text: Definition 5.1. A descriptive statistic is a numerical summary of a data set, computed directly from the values in that data set. An inferential statistic uses a data set as a sample from a larger population and makes a claim about that population's value; every inferential claim carries an associated margin of error.

Margin noteThe two-way split between descriptive and inferential statistics was formalized by John Tukey in the 1960s, but the underlying distinction goes back at least to the work of Gauss on errors of observation in the early 1800s. Tukey's contribution was the modern vocabulary of exploratory and confirmatory data analysis, which roughly maps onto the descriptive and inferential traditions.

Five terms recur throughout this chapter. A data set is the actual collection of numbers in hand: 10 weights, 12 class sizes, 7 dog measurements. A population is the full group whose properties we would ideally like to know: all American voters, all 11th-grade boys, all dogs in a city. Usually a population is too large to measure directly. A sample is a subset of the population that we can measure: a poll of 1,000 voters, a class of 25 students. A descriptive claim is a statement about the data set itself; an inferential claim is a statement about the population, supported by the data set as a sample.
Descriptive statistics describe the data we have. Inferential statistics infer claims about data we do not have, from data we do.

Common mistakeTreating an inferential claim as if it were exact. When a poll reports “45%, ±3%,” the ±3% is part of the claim — not optional fine print. Dropping it converts a careful inferential statement into an overconfident descriptive-sounding one, and creates a false impression of precision the underlying data does not support. Inferential claims always carry margins of error; reporting them without that margin misrepresents the math.

Example 5.1. Same number, two claims.

A high school basketball coach measures the height of every player on the team. The 15 players average 6 feet 1 inch. The coach posts the average on the team bulletin board, and a few days later tells a reporter, “The average high school basketball player in our state is about 6 feet 1.” Classify each of these two statements as descriptive or inferential.

Solution.

The bulletin-board statement reports the average height of the 15 players on the team. That is a summary of the data set itself (the 15 measurements). It is exact for that team and carries no margin of error: descriptive.

The statement to the reporter uses the same 15 measurements but stretches them to a claim about the population of all high-school basketball players in the state — a group of thousands, of whom the team is one sample. The stretch is the inferential move. An honest version of the same claim would include a margin of error: inferential.

Notice that the same data and the same numerical answer (6 feet 1) power both statements. The difference is not in the computation; it is in what is being claimed.

In the wildEvery news headline reporting a survey, poll, or scientific study is making an inferential claim, and every such headline should be read with the ± in mind. “Voters favor candidate X by 6 points” sounds decisive, but if the margin of error is ±4%, the actual statistical statement is “the gap is between 2 and 10 points” — which is much less decisive. Major polling organizations publish the margin of error alongside their headline numbers; popular reporting often does not, which is one of the most common sources of statistical misunderstanding in public life.

1. Classify each as descriptive or inferential: “The 28 students in this class averaged 84% on the midterm.”

    Show answer

    Answer. Descriptive. The claim summarizes the data set (the 28 midterm scores); no extrapolation to a larger group.

  2. Classify: “52% of likely U.S. voters favor the candidate, ±3%.” The poll surveyed 1,200 people.

    Show answer

    Answer. Inferential. The 1,200 people are a sample; the claim is about the much larger population of likely U.S. voters, with a margin of error attached.

  3. A drug company tests its new medicine on 200 volunteers and reports: “Patients in the trial experienced an average 15% reduction in symptoms.” Descriptive or inferential?

    Show answer

    Answer. Descriptive. The claim is bounded to the 200 trial participants, who are the data set itself. If the company said “this drug reduces symptoms by 15% in patients” without the qualifier, that would be an inferential claim.

  4. Identify the population and the sample in the following scenario: a market researcher surveys 500 grocery shoppers in Phoenix and reports that 38% prefer Brand A over Brand B.

    Show answer

    Answer. The sample is the 500 surveyed shoppers; the population is (depending on the researcher's intended claim) all grocery shoppers in Phoenix, or possibly all grocery shoppers nationally.

  5. True or false: a descriptive statistic always carries a margin of error.

    Show answer

    Answer. False. Descriptive statistics are exact for the data set they describe. Margins of error attach to inferential statistics.

  6. A nutrition study weighs every student in a specific high school and reports the mean weight as 152 pounds. The principal then tells the school board, “The average high-school student in our city weighs about 152 pounds.” What kind of claim is the principal making, and what is missing from it?

    Show answer

    Answer. The principal is making an inferential claim — extending a result from one school to all high-school students in the city. The honest version of the claim should include a margin of error, since the school may not be representative of the city as a whole.

content.title: Descriptive or inferential?
content.problem: Classify each claim as **descriptive** or **inferential**: (a) "The average age of the students in this classroom is 19." (b) "The average age of all undergraduates in the U.S. is 21." (c) "In our survey of 1,000 voters, 53% support the new bill." (d) "Among all American voters, support for the bill is around 53%, with a margin of error of ±3%."
content.solution:
**Descriptive** = summarizes a specific data set. **Inferential** = claims about a larger population based on a sample.

(a) **Descriptive** — "the students in this classroom" is the whole population in question.

(b) **Inferential** — claiming the average for *all* undergraduates, more than we can measure directly.

(c) **Descriptive** — "in our survey of 1,000 voters" is the specific data set.

(d) **Inferential** — the margin of error gives it away. The claim extends the 53% finding to *all American voters*.

*Key tell.* Inferential claims usually have a margin of error, a confidence level, or a word like "all" / "population" / "estimated."

content.title: Same number, different claim
content.problem: A class of 30 students has an average exam score of 78. Write one **descriptive** claim and one **inferential** claim about this data.
content.solution:
**Descriptive (about the actual class):**

*"The 30 students in this class scored an average of 78 on the exam."*

**Inferential (about a larger population, like "all students who take this course"):**

*"Based on a sample of 30 students, we estimate that students taking this course score around 78 on average, with a margin of error that depends on the sample size and variability."*

*The takeaway.* Same number, two completely different kinds of claim. The descriptive version is exact for the data we have. The inferential version is an estimate, and it needs the language of uncertainty (margin of error, confidence) to be honest.

content.title: Summary
content.content: Descriptive statistics summarize the data we have in hand. Inferential statistics extend a finding from a sample to a larger population we can't measure directly. The math overlaps; the *claim* is what differs. Inferential claims always carry a margin of error or confidence interval — descriptive claims do not.

content.text: With the descriptive-vs-inferential vocabulary in hand, the rest of the chapter is a structured tour.
